skill.name: fda-database
skill.description: Query openFDA data for drugs, devices, foods, veterinary products, recalls, adverse events, approvals, and substance identifiers.
skill.summary: Access openFDA regulatory data with Python helpers, examples, and endpoint references.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, credential, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns plus a critical heuristic. Manual review found no prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, or executable shell use; most findings are documentation examples or false positives. The skill does ship Python scripts that call api.fda.gov, read an optional FDA_API_KEY, and write a local response cache, so it should publish with a medium warning.

medium_findings.title: Expected Network Access to openFDA
medium_findings.description: The Python helper builds requests to https://api.fda.gov and sends optional query parameters, including an API key when provided. This is core skill behavior, but users should understand that prompts may trigger external FDA API calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request destination is clearly the official openFDA base URL, and requests.get is used directly. No evidence found of requests to unrelated domains.

medium_findings.title: Local Response Cache Writes
medium_findings.description: The helper creates a local fda_cache directory and writes API responses to JSON cache files. This is not malicious, but cached regulatory results may persist on disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and confirmed in the cache implementation. The writes are scoped to cache files generated from request parameters.

low_findings.title: Optional FDA_API_KEY Environment Access
low_findings.description: The scripts read FDA_API_KEY to increase openFDA rate limits and pass it as an openFDA request parameter. No evidence found that the key is logged or sent to non-FDA endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable read is explicit and limited to FDA_API_KEY. Review found no secondary network destination or credential harvesting behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings occur inside Markdown examples or inline code references. They are documentation snippets and not executable code paths in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines are Markdown fences, inline URLs, or documentation examples. No Ruby runtime or shell backtick execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Flags Are Mostly Domain-Text False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto matches largely come from words such as desc, CAS, DNA, RNA, and medical field names. The only real MD5 use generates non-security cache filenames.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual samples show non-security text matches and one cache-key hash. MD5 is not used for password storage, signatures, or integrity claims.

dangerous_patterns.title: External HTTPS Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper performs outbound HTTPS requests to api.fda.gov through requests.get.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code has a fixed openFDA base URL and a direct requests.get call. This is intended functionality, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cache File Creation and Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper creates a local cache directory and writes API response JSON files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cache implementation directly creates the directory and writes JSON files. The behavior is visible and limited to response caching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Read
dangerous_patterns.description: Example entry points read FDA_API_KEY from the environment for openFDA authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Only FDA_API_KEY was found in the reviewed runtime paths. No evidence found of broad environment dumping or secret enumeration.

content.value_statement: FDA safety and regulatory data is spread across many openFDA endpoints. This skill provides Python helpers and references for targeted FDA queries.
actual_capabilities: Query drug adverse events, labels, NDC records, recalls, approvals, and shortages.
Search medical device events, recalls, classifications, 510(k) clearances, PMA records, and UDI data.
Monitor food recalls and food adverse event reports with focused search terms.
Analyze animal and veterinary adverse events by species, breed, drug, and reaction.
Look up substances by UNII, CAS-related codes, names, and molecular relationships.
Use rate limiting, pagination, error handling, and optional local response caching.
limitations: Results depend on public openFDA data and may not include every FDA record.
The skill is for research support and does not provide medical, legal, or regulatory advice.
An FDA API key is optional, but unauthenticated usage has lower daily limits.
Queries require careful field selection because each openFDA endpoint has different schemas.

use_cases.title: Drug Safety Review
use_cases.description: Compare adverse event counts, common reactions, serious reports, labels, and recall records for a drug.

use_cases.title: Device Market Surveillance
use_cases.description: Track device adverse events, recalls, classifications, clearances, and UDI records across FDA databases.

use_cases.title: Food Recall Monitoring
use_cases.description: Search openFDA enforcement reports for allergen, contamination, product, date, and classification patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Drug Label
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the FDA database skill to find current openFDA label information for Lipitor and summarize the indications and warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Review Recent Recalls
prompt_templates.prompt: Search openFDA for Class I recalls involving metformin. Summarize product names, recall reasons, dates, and status.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Device Signals
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare adverse event and recall patterns for pacemakers and insulin pumps. Note event types and important data limitations.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Cross-Endpoint Study
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an openFDA query workflow for acetaminophen that covers adverse events, labels, recalls, shortages, and substance identifiers.

output_examples.input: Summarize aspirin adverse event patterns from openFDA.
output_examples.output: A concise safety profile with total reports, common reaction terms, serious event counts, recall notes, and data caveats.

output_examples.input: Check FDA device data for a pacemaker product.
output_examples.output: A surveillance summary covering adverse events, event types, recall records, classifications, and relevant identifiers.

output_examples.input: Find food recalls related to undeclared peanuts.
output_examples.output: A recall brief listing affected products, firms, recall classes, distribution notes, report dates, and recall reasons.

best_practices: Use exact endpoint fields and date filters to reduce broad or noisy openFDA searches.
Set FDA_API_KEY for higher limits, and avoid sharing the key in prompts or reports.
Cross-check important findings with official FDA source pages before making decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat adverse event counts as incidence rates without denominator data.
Do not use broad wildcard searches when a field-specific query is available.
Do not present openFDA output as medical or regulatory advice without expert review.

faq.question: Does this skill require an FDA API key?
faq.answer: No. An API key is optional, but it raises the openFDA daily request limit.

faq.question: Which FDA databases does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers drugs, devices, foods, animal and veterinary data, substances, recalls, adverse events, and approvals.

faq.question: Does it store data locally?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python helper can cache openFDA responses in local JSON files unless caching is disabled.

faq.question: Can it provide medical advice?
faq.answer: No. It supports research workflows and should not replace clinical or regulatory judgment.

faq.question: Can it compare products across endpoints?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show drug safety profiles, device surveillance, recall checks, and cross-database lookups.

faq.question: Is all FDA data available through openFDA?
faq.answer: No. openFDA contains public datasets, and coverage varies by endpoint and reporting source.
